**Grand Valley State University** (**GVSU**, **GV**, or **Grand Valley**) is a [public university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in [Allendale, Michigan](/wiki/Allendale_Charter_Township,_Michigan "Allendale Charter Township, Michigan"), United States.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was established in 1960 as **Grand Valley State College**. Its main campus is situated on 1,322 acres (5.35 km2) approximately 12 miles (19 km) west of [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan").[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) The university also features campuses in Grand Rapids and [Holland](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan") and regional centers in [Battle Creek](/wiki/Battle_Creek,_Michigan "Battle Creek, Michigan"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Muskegon](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan"), and [Traverse City](/wiki/Traverse_City,_Michigan "Traverse City, Michigan").

GVSU enrolled more than 22,000 students as of fall 2025 from 82 of 83 Michigan counties and dozens of other states and foreign countries.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)[[9]](#cite_note-Administration-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) It employed nearly 4,000 people, with about 1,800 faculty and 2,000 staff.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)[[11]](#cite_note-GVDetroit-11)[[12]](#cite_note-forbes-12) The university has alumni from 50 U.S. states, Canada, and 25 other countries.[[13]](#cite_note-alumni-13)

GVSU's [NCAA Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") sports teams are the [Lakers](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_Lakers "Grand Valley State Lakers") and they compete in the [Great Lakes Intercollegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Conference "Great Lakes Intercollegiate Athletic Conference") (GLIAC) in all 20 intercollegiate varsity sports.[[14]](#cite_note-NACDA-14) They have won 31 NCAA Division II National Championships.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## History

### Formation, planning and construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation, planning and construction")]

In 1958 the [Michigan Legislature](/wiki/Michigan_Legislature "Michigan Legislature") commissioned a study that demonstrated a need for a four-year college in the [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") area, Michigan's second largest metropolitan region. Local businessman [Bill Seidman](/wiki/L._William_Seidman "L. William Seidman") created a committee to study the report and spearhead the planning and promotion to create such an institution. In the following year the Michigan Legislature established the college. A naming contest was held, and out of 2500 submissions, "Grand Valley State College" was chosen. Private donations, including $350,000 to purchase land and $1,000,000 for construction, were secured from 5,000 individuals, organizations, and business throughout [West Michigan](/wiki/West_Michigan "West Michigan"). In 1961, the Grand Valley State College Board of Control chose a 876-acre (3.55 km2) site in [Ottawa County](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Michigan "Ottawa County, Michigan") near the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") for the new campus, and construction of academic buildings began the following year.[[2]](#cite_note-History-2)[[19]](#cite_note-SeidmanGRPressObit-19)

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

Grand Valley State College accepted its first class of 225 students in 1963, Diane (Hatch) Paton was the first student, and was among the first graduation of 138 students on June 18, 1967. The middle-late 1960s saw the addition of the first dormitories and construction of new academic buildings, including the Zumberge Library, named for the university's first president, [James Zumberge](/wiki/James_Zumberge "James Zumberge").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1969, the *[Grand Valley Lanthorn](/wiki/Grand_Valley_Lanthorn "Grand Valley Lanthorn")* printed an issue containing several vulgarities and obscenities. After complaints from some at Grand Valley State College and the surrounding communities, the [Ottawa County, Michigan](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Michigan "Ottawa County, Michigan"), sheriff arrested the editor, and the prosecutor closed down the newspaper office. The university, then a co-ed college, sued the sheriff and prosecutor for closing the Lanthorn offices. Eventually, [Michigan's Attorney General](/wiki/Michigan_Attorney_General "Michigan Attorney General") settled the case out of court, siding with the college[[22]](#cite_note-ivlubberslanthorn-22) because the Lanthorn's content was considered covered by [Freedom of Speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_Speech "Freedom of Speech").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the 1970s Grand Valley organized its academic units into several colleges: College of Arts and Sciences, Thomas Jefferson College, William James College, Seidman College of Business, and College IV. Michigan Governor [William Milliken](/wiki/William_Milliken "William Milliken") signed the law changing the institution's name to **Grand Valley State Colleges** in 1973. However, the "s" was dropped and the name was reverted to Grand Valley State College in 1983 when the academic programs were reorganized into divisions.[[2]](#cite_note-History-2)

### College to comprehensive university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: College to comprehensive university")]

In 1987 the Michigan Legislature passed a law renaming the college to Grand Valley State University.[[23]](#cite_note-Act_21-23) The 1980s and 1990s saw addition of satellite campuses or centers in downtown Grand Rapids, Muskegon, Holland, and Traverse City. In 2004, the university's board reorganized the university structure again into a college system consisting of the College of Liberal Arts and Sciences, College of Interdisciplinary Studies, College of Community and Public Service, College of Education, College of Health Professions, [Kirkhof College of Nursing](/wiki/Kirkhof_College_of_Nursing "Kirkhof College of Nursing"), Seidman College of Business, and Seymour and Esther Padnos College of Engineering and Computing.[[2]](#cite_note-History-2) Grand Valley completed its first 50 years with a comprehensive campaign that raised almost $100 million from over 17,000 donors, making it the university's largest campaign to date. Money raised during the campaign has helped fund many construction projects on campus, including the Mary Idema Pew Library and the L. William Seidman Center.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Continued growth beyond 50th anniversary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Continued growth beyond 50th anniversary")]

In 2012, GVSU announced several more construction projects and land purchases.[[25]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-25) Future buildings to be constructed include a new biology laboratory building and an addition and renovation to the Zumberge Library on the Allendale campus. Land purchases in 2012 included property in downtown Grand Rapids adjacent to the medical mile for healthcare program expansion, which would become the Daniel and Pamella Devos Center for Interprofessional Health. In 2013, GVSU announced it would add on to Au Sable Hall and construct a building to house the GVSU Laker Store (known as University Bookstore prior to April 2015),[[26]](#cite_note-GVSULanthorn-26) with expanded dining facilities.[[27]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-27)

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Campuses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Cook_Carillon_Tower.JPG/250px-Cook_Carillon_Tower.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cook_Carillon_Tower.JPG)

[Cook Carillon Tower](/wiki/Cook_Carillon_Tower "Cook Carillon Tower") at Grand Valley State University's Allendale campus

Grand Valley has three primary campuses. The main campus is located in [Allendale Charter Township, Michigan](/wiki/Allendale_Charter_Township,_Michigan "Allendale Charter Township, Michigan"), about 15 miles (24 km) west of [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"); two satellite campuses are located in Grand Rapids. The university has regional academic centers in Detroit, Holland, Muskegon, and Traverse City.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/The_Rapid_Laker_Line.jpg/250px-The_Rapid_Laker_Line.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Rapid_Laker_Line.jpg)

Laker Line bus at Kirkhof Center on the Allendale campus

[The Rapid](/wiki/The_Rapid "The Rapid") operates bus service between the three primary campuses, funded by the university. The [Laker Line](/wiki/Laker_Line "Laker Line"), named for GVSU's sports teams, connects all three campuses via [Lake Michigan Drive](/wiki/M-45_(Michigan_highway) "M-45 (Michigan highway)"), and additional shuttle services operate on the Allendale campus. These bus services are open to the public, and rides on all The Rapid services are free for Grand Valley students, faculty and staff on all Rapid routes with a valid I.D. card.[[28]](#cite_note-gvsubus-28)

### Allendale campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Allendale campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/GVSU_Lake_Michigan_Hall.JPG/250px-GVSU_Lake_Michigan_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Lake_Michigan_Hall.JPG)

Lake Michigan Hall was one of GVSU's first buildings.

The university's main and original campus in Allendale is the site of most of the university's programs. The Allendale campus is composed of 1,322 acres perched above the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Michigan) "Grand River (Michigan)") next to a system of [ravines](/wiki/Ravine "Ravine") and is divided into two areas, north and south campus, separated by West Campus Drive.[[29]](#cite_note-overview-29)[[30]](#cite_note-maps-30) State highway [M-45](/wiki/M-45_(Michigan_highway) "M-45 (Michigan highway)") links the campus in suburban Allendale to [US Highway 31](/wiki/U.S._Route_31_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 31 in Michigan")/[Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to the west and [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") to the east. [Lubbers Stadium](/wiki/Lubbers_Stadium "Lubbers Stadium"), the [GVSU Fieldhouse](/wiki/GVSU_Fieldhouse "GVSU Fieldhouse") and all other athletic facilities for the school's 20 varsity sports are also on the Allendale campus. The campus is dotted with many sculptures, including works by [Dale Eldred](/wiki/Dale_Eldred "Dale Eldred") and [James Clover](/wiki/James_Clover "James Clover"). Academic facilities on the Allendale campus includes at least 122 classrooms, 144 research laboratories, 20 lab prep rooms, 21 computer labs, and the Mary Idema Pew Library Learning and Information Commons.[[31]](#cite_note-thecampuses-31) The Allendale campus is also home to the Kindschi Hall of Science, a science facility that opened in the fall of 2015.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The Holton-Hooker Learning and Living Center, with space for 490 students, opened in August 2016. The building is [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") silver-certified.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Robert C. Pew Grand Rapids campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Robert C. Pew Grand Rapids campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/GVSU_Pew_Campus.jpg/250px-GVSU_Pew_Campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Pew_Campus.jpg)

Robert C. Pew Grand Rapids campus

The 65-acre (260,000 m2) [Robert C. Pew](/wiki/Robert_Pew "Robert Pew") Grand Rapids campus is in the heart of downtown Grand Rapids on the banks of the Grand River.[[29]](#cite_note-overview-29)[[31]](#cite_note-thecampuses-31)[[30]](#cite_note-maps-30) It consists of 11 buildings and three leased spaces and includes the [Richard DeVos](/wiki/Richard_DeVos "Richard DeVos") Center, [L.V. Eberhard](/wiki/Eberhard%27s "Eberhard's") Center, L. William Seidman Center, [Beckering Family Carillon Tower](/wiki/Beckering_Family_Carillon_Tower "Beckering Family Carillon Tower"), The Depot (houses the Michigan Small Business and Technology Development headquarters), [Hauenstein](/wiki/Ralph_Hauenstein "Ralph Hauenstein") Center for Presidential Studies, Keller Engineering Laboratories, John C. Kennedy Hall of Engineering, [Peter F. Secchia](/wiki/Peter_F._Secchia "Peter F. Secchia") Hall (housing), Winter Hall (housing), and the [Van Andel](/wiki/Jay_Van_Andel "Jay Van Andel") Global Trade Center. Winter Hall is typically occupied by graduate students and is fully furnished along with appliances. These facilities include 57 classrooms, 78 research laboratories, 23 lab prep rooms, 11 computer labs, and the Steelcase Library.[[31]](#cite_note-thecampuses-31) The Steelcase Library was renovated in the Summer of 2025 to include a University Testing Center and Psychology Lab.

### Health campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Health campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg/250px-GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Cook_Devos.jpg)

The Cook-DeVos Center for Health Science is part of the Health Campus in downtown Grand Rapids

The 14.5-acre (59,000 m2) Health Campus is a part of the [Grand Rapids Medical Mile](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Medical_Mile "Grand Rapids Medical Mile") area and houses many of GVSU's health programs. The Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences (CHS), which opened in 2003, reached capacity in 2011.[[34]](#cite_note-Reister-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) To accommodate the growth in the health sciences, GVSU completed the $37.5 million Raleigh J. Finkelstein Hall north of the Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences,[[36]](#cite_note-36) and in 2018 broke ground on the next phase, the Daniel and Pamella DeVos Center for Interprofessional Health, a $70 million building that is attached to the Cook-DeVos Center for Health Sciences and includes a parking structure shared with nearby [Spectrum Health](/wiki/Spectrum_Health "Spectrum Health").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Regional centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Regional centers")]

GVSU has three locations in [Muskegon, Michigan](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan"). The James L. Stevenson Center for Higher Education at [Muskegon Community College](/wiki/Muskegon_Community_College "Muskegon Community College") was established in the fall of 1995 as a joint venture between GVSU, [Ferris State University](/wiki/Ferris_State_University "Ferris State University"), and [Western Michigan University](/wiki/Western_Michigan_University "Western Michigan University").[[2]](#cite_note-History-2) The center offers several GVSU graduate and undergraduate programs.[[38]](#cite_note-mccsche-38) Lake Michigan Center houses the [Annis Water Resources Institute](/wiki/Annis_Water_Resources_Institute "Annis Water Resources Institute") (AWRI).[[39]](#cite_note-wboutawri-39) The [Michigan Alternative and Renewable Energy Center](/wiki/Michigan_Alternative_and_Renewable_Energy_Center "Michigan Alternative and Renewable Energy Center") (MAREC), which is the first fully integrated demonstration facility for distributed generation of electricity using alternative and renewable energy technologies in the United States.[[40]](#cite_note-marec-40)[[41]](#cite_note-The_Chief_Engineer-41)

The Traverse City Regional Center was established in the fall of 1995 in [Traverse City, Michigan](/wiki/Traverse_City,_Michigan "Traverse City, Michigan"), and is at the NMC University Center in partnership with [Northwestern Michigan College](/wiki/Northwestern_Michigan_College "Northwestern Michigan College"). The center offers undergraduate and graduate degrees in education, social work, and liberal studies.[[2]](#cite_note-History-2)[[42]](#cite_note-Traverse-42)
The GVSU Physician Assistant program enrolls 10-14 students at the TC campus.

The Meijer campus, just outside downtown [Holland, Michigan](/wiki/Holland,_Michigan "Holland, Michigan"), was opened in 1998 and was named for the [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer") family for their donation of land. The campus houses continuing education programs in 30,693 square feet (2,851.5 m2) of building space and contains 12 classrooms, 2 conference rooms, 3 labs and 11 offices.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

The Detroit Center was established in 2012 when GVSU purchased the Barden Building adjacent to [Comerica Park](/wiki/Comerica_Park "Comerica Park") in downtown [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") to house its charter school offices.[[11]](#cite_note-GVDetroit-11) The center also houses the Southeast Michigan Region of the Michigan Small Business & Technology Development Center, of which GVSU is the supervisor.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/GVSU_Student_Services_Building.JPG/250px-GVSU_Student_Services_Building.JPG)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Student_Services_Building.JPG)

Student Services Building at Grand Valley State University-Allendale campus

Grand Valley State University is governed by an eight-member board of trustees, whose members are appointed by the [Governor of Michigan](/wiki/Governor_of_Michigan "Governor of Michigan") and confirmed by the [Michigan Senate](/wiki/Michigan_Senate "Michigan Senate") for terms of eight years. This setup is provided for by the constitution of the state of Michigan of 1963. Members of the board serve without compensation. The board appoints the president of the university, formulates university policies, controls university finances, and acts as the supreme governing body of the institution. The president of the university administers the policies of the board of trustees.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Finance")]

GVSU's general fund budget is $351 million, of which $275 million is from tuition and $72 million is from state appropriations. As of 2019, Grand Valley's amount of university-based student financial aid was $289 million, including $90 million of that in scholarships and grants.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) During the 2013–2014 academic year, full-time dependent students received an average award of $13,276.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public safety")]

The Grand Valley Police Department provides law enforcement services for the Allendale Campus. While the department is self-empowered to enforce its jurisdiction, officers are also deputized by the [Ottawa County](/wiki/Ottawa_County,_Michigan "Ottawa County, Michigan") [Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff "Sheriff")'s Department. Because [Allendale](/wiki/Allendale_Charter_Township,_Michigan "Allendale Charter Township, Michigan") doesn't have a police department, the Grand Valley State University Police can handle cases anywhere in Ottawa County, mainly in Allendale and the area surrounding the campus. The department handles other security issues, such as parking and driving violations, community policing, as well lost and found. Allendale Fire Department serves the campus. The Department of Public Safety also employs several students who assist the department by performing a variety of clerical and security based duties and services.[[47]](#cite_note-47)
The Pew Campus Security and Regional Centers is an entity distinct from the Department of Public safety, and handles security and public safety issues for the Grand Rapids Pew Campus and all Regional Centers including the Holland (MI) Meijer Campus, the Muskegon (MI) campus and the Traverse City (MI) campus. This department is not sworn or certified and relies on the Grand Rapids Police Department and other local law enforcement agencies for official law enforcement when necessary.[[48]](#cite_note-pewsec-48)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Glenn_A._Niemeyer_Learning_and_Living_Center.JPG/250px-Glenn_A._Niemeyer_Learning_and_Living_Center.JPG)](/wiki/File:Glenn_A._Niemeyer_Learning_and_Living_Center.JPG)

Glenn A. Niemeyer Learning and Living Center is home to the Frederik Meijer Honors College, and is located at GVSU-Allendale campus.

Grand Valley State University is a large, primarily residential comprehensive university that has a large undergraduate enrollment and emphasis.[[49]](#cite_note-49) GVSU offers over 200 areas of study, including 82 undergraduate majors and 36 graduate programs that include 74 graduate emphases and certificate programs[[31]](#cite_note-thecampuses-31)[[50]](#cite_note-academicprograms-50) at the certificate, [bachelor's](/wiki/Bachelor%27s "Bachelor's"), post-bachelor's, [master's](/wiki/Master%27s "Master's"), post-master's, and [doctoral](/wiki/Doctoral "Doctoral") levels.[[51]](#cite_note-princetonreview-51) GVSU conferred 4,448 undergraduate degrees and 1,033 graduate degrees in 2012–2013.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Its most popular undergraduate majors, by 2021 graduates, were:[[53]](#cite_note-53)

:   :   Biomedical Sciences (262)
    :   Health Professions and Related Clinical Sciences (250)
    :   Exercise Science and Kinesiology (250)
    :   Marketing/Marketing Management (226)
    :   Psychology (226)
    :   Registered Nursing/Registered Nurse (208)
    :   Finance (205)

Grand Valley's student body consists of 21,636 undergraduates and 3,458 graduate students across all campuses and centers with the majority being on the Allendale campus (as of Fall 2014).[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) The fall 2014 incoming freshman undergraduate class of 4,199 students, represented 80 Michigan counties, 23 states, and 20 countries. 86% of first year students live on campus: 3,591 freshman chose to live on-campus in fall 2014, while 608 chose to live off-campus.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)[[54]](#cite_note-ia-54) As of fall 2014, more than 400 international students were enrolled at the university representing 82 countries.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)

Grand Valley is accredited by the [Higher Learning Commission](/wiki/Higher_Learning_Commission "Higher Learning Commission").[[55]](#cite_note-catfacts-55) Grand Valley also has baccalaureate program accreditation with [AACSB](/wiki/AACSB "AACSB"), [ABET](/wiki/ABET "ABET"), [APTA](/wiki/American_Physical_Therapy_Association "American Physical Therapy Association"), [CSWE](/wiki/Council_on_Social_Work_Education "Council on Social Work Education"), [NASAD](/wiki/NASAD "NASAD"), [NASM](/wiki/National_Association_of_Schools_of_Music "National Association of Schools of Music"), [NCATE](/wiki/NCATE "NCATE"), and [NLN](/wiki/National_League_for_Nursing "National League for Nursing").

### Colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Colleges")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/GVSU_Lake_Ontario_Hall.JPG/250px-GVSU_Lake_Ontario_Hall.JPG)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Lake_Ontario_Hall.JPG)

Lake Ontario Hall is home to the Brooks College of Interdisciplinary Studies, and is part of the Great Lakes Complex.

The university consists of seven degree-granting colleges:[[2]](#cite_note-History-2)

* [F.E. Seidman College of Business](/wiki/Seidman_College_of_Business "Seidman College of Business")
* College of Education and Community Innovation[[56]](#cite_note-56)
* College of Liberal Arts and Sciences[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* Seymour and Esther Padnos College of Engineering and Computing[[58]](#cite_note-58)
* College of Health Professions[[59]](#cite_note-59)
* [Kirkhof College of Nursing](/wiki/Kirkhof_College_of_Nursing "Kirkhof College of Nursing")
* Brooks College of Interdisciplinary Studies[[60]](#cite_note-60)

GVSU is also home to the [Frederik Meijer](/wiki/Frederik_Meijer "Frederik Meijer") Honors College,[[61]](#cite_note-61) which is non-degree-granting but is meant to provide a more challenging interdisciplinary education in a learning-living environment. The honors college is in the Glenn A. Niemeyer Learning and Living Center on the south side of the Allendale campus.[[62]](#cite_note-honorscollege-62)

GVSU has a [National Association of Schools of Art and Design](/wiki/National_Association_of_Schools_of_Art_and_Design "National Association of Schools of Art and Design") (NASAD) accredited art program,[[63]](#cite_note-NASAD-63) including emphases in [illustration](/wiki/Illustration "Illustration"), [graphic design](/wiki/Graphic_design "Graphic design"), [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic_art "Ceramic art"), [printmaking](/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking"), painting, [visual studies](/wiki/Visual_studies "Visual studies"), and sculpture. Art students attend classes at the Calder Art Center, named for contemporary artist [Alexander Calder](/wiki/Alexander_Calder "Alexander Calder"). The building includes exhibition space under the name of the Stuart & Barbara Padnos Art Gallery.[[64]](#cite_note-bfa-64)

### Study abroad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Study abroad")]

The Padnos International Center collaborates with students who wish to [study abroad](/wiki/Study_abroad "Study abroad").[[65]](#cite_note-picsa-65) Grand Valley has more than 4,000 study abroad programs, both affiliated with and independent from GVSU, from which students can choose.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)

In 1995 Peimin Ni ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 倪培民; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Ní Péimín*) established GVSU's Shanghai, China study abroad program, and by the 2000s he and Geling Shang (商戈令; *Shāng Gēlìng*) co-lead it. Word of mouth and announcements in classes were used to promote it. [Daniel Golden](/wiki/Daniel_Golden "Daniel Golden"), author of *[Spy Schools: How the CIA, FBI, and Foreign Intelligence Secretly Exploit America's Universities](/wiki/Spy_Schools:_How_the_CIA,_FBI,_and_Foreign_Intelligence_Secretly_Exploit_America%27s_Universities "Spy Schools: How the CIA, FBI, and Foreign Intelligence Secretly Exploit America's Universities")*, stated that the organizers initially had trouble finding interested students.[[66]](#cite_note-Goldenp120-66)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Richard_M._DeVos_Center.jpg/250px-Richard_M._DeVos_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Richard_M._DeVos_Center.jpg)

Richard M. DeVos Center on GVSU-Pew campus

During the two years prior to October 2009 university researchers engaged in over 186 research projects funded by more than $32.7 million in grants.[[80]](#cite_note-talkingpoints-80) Grand Valley's [Annis Water Resources Institute](/wiki/Annis_Water_Resources_Institute "Annis Water Resources Institute") conducts research on water resources, including: ecosystem structure and function, contaminants and toxicology, hydrology, land use, watershed, stream, and wetland ecology, water quality, and basic and applied limnology.[[39]](#cite_note-wboutawri-39) GVSU's [Michigan Alternative and Renewable Energy Center](/wiki/Michigan_Alternative_and_Renewable_Energy_Center "Michigan Alternative and Renewable Energy Center") (MAREC) recently received funding for its $3.7 million Lake Michigan Offshore Wind Assessment Project to test wind energy on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). The research should take three years as a floating buoy will be used and moved to collect data from different locations on the lake.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The [Michigan State University College of Human Medicine](/wiki/Michigan_State_University_College_of_Human_Medicine "Michigan State University College of Human Medicine") maintains the Secchia Center, a medical campus in downtown Grand Rapids.[[82]](#cite_note-fox17secchia-82)[[83]](#cite_note-msugrcampus-83) Along with GVSU and two Grand Rapids hospitals, it is a founding member of the Grand Rapids Medical Education Partners. This partnership allows "educational opportunities for residents, fellows, practicing physicians, physician assistants, nurses and students in other health professions."[[84]](#cite_note-grmepabout-84)

The Early Assurance Program reserves spaces in MSU's medical program for top-performing GVSU pre-medical graduates. One of the program's goals is to select first-generation college students, students from underprivileged areas, and students who have expressed a desire to work in high-demand medical specialties. Students entering the program must agree to work in under-served areas after they complete their medical degrees. The first group of six students entered this program during the fall 2010 semester.[[85]](#cite_note-Tagliavia-85)[[86]](#cite_note-Kelley-86)

The two universities have announced a joint program specializing in [clinical trial](/wiki/Clinical_trial "Clinical trial") management, aimed at providing the foundations for carrying out clinical drug trials in West Michigan. It is an [online](/wiki/E-learning "E-learning") certificate program, and was started due to requests from local drug trial companies. The program was set to begin for the fall semester of 2011.[[87]](#cite_note-GVSUMSUpartners-87)[[88]](#cite_note-mSanchez-88)

## Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Mary_Idema_Pew_Library_at_Grand_Valley_State_University_%28main_campus%29_in_Allendale%2C_Michigan.jpg/330px-Mary_Idema_Pew_Library_at_Grand_Valley_State_University_%28main_campus%29_in_Allendale%2C_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary_Idema_Pew_Library_at_Grand_Valley_State_University_(main_campus)_in_Allendale,_Michigan.jpg)

Mary Idema Pew Library at the Allendale campus

Each [academic library](/wiki/Academic_library "Academic library")'s collection is tailored to its location and the programs it serves, with daily deliveries between sites.[[89]](#cite_note-gvsulibhrs-89)

* Mary Idema Pew Library (Allendale)
* Lemmen Library & Archives (Allendale)
* Steelcase Library (Grand Rapids)
* Frey Foundation Learning Center (Grand Rapids)
* Curriculum Materials Library (Grand Rapids)

The university libraries offer opportunities for research, collaboration, and individual study. Collectively, the libraries subscribe to over 60,000 print and electronic journals. They also house more than 1,482,633 books including more than 829,463 electronic books.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)

Construction the Mary Idema Pew Library on the Allendale campus was finished in 2013.[[90]](#cite_note-Timeline-90) The building is named after the late Mary Idema Pew.[[91]](#cite_note-MurrayD-91) The $70 million, 140,000-square-foot (13,000 m2) facility holds 150,000 books. It also has an automated storage and retrieval system that can handle 600,000 volumes.[[92]](#cite_note-Mary_Idema_Pew_Library_Collections-92) The [U.S. Department of Energy](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Energy "U.S. Department of Energy") announced the library used part of the $21 million allocated for technical assistance projects to improve energy use in commercial buildings. The library was one of only 24 projects in the United States receiving the assistantships that was funded by the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act").[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Collections")]

Grand Valley is a recipient of United States government documents and receives 44% of the documents distributed by the [Federal Depository Library Program](/wiki/Federal_Depository_Library_Program "Federal Depository Library Program").[[94]](#cite_note-govresandmaps-94)

The Curriculum Materials Library, part of the Steelcase Library at the Robert C. Pew campus, houses instructional materials for preschool through grade twelve and provides spaces where education majors can preview resources, develop lesson plans, create media for the classroom, and try out teaching aides.[[95]](#cite_note-cmlgvsu-95)

The Lemmen Library & Archives on the Allendale campus houses the University Archives, which include extensive collections of rare books and Michigan novels, the Harvey Lemmen Collection on [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), the [Young Lords](/wiki/Young_Lords "Young Lords") project on line at Young Lords in Lincoln Park [[1]](http://www.gvsu.edu/younglords), the largest Latino oral history collection in the Mid-West by [Jose Cha Cha Jimenez](/wiki/Jose_Cha_Cha_Jimenez "Jose Cha Cha Jimenez"), and the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and the papers of acclaimed Michigan author [Jim Harrison](/wiki/Jim_Harrison "Jim Harrison").[[96]](#cite_note-scua-96) There is also an [Anthony Powell](/wiki/Anthony_Powell "Anthony Powell") collection, consisting of the many editions of *Dance to the Music of Time*, his other novels, a complete run of the Anthony Powell Society Newsletters and its journal, *Secret Harmonies*.

A collection of over 15,000 works of art features public sculpture, prints and drawings, American Impressionism, Aboriginal art, Indian art, world photography, contemporary art, and more. Most of the collection is on view and can be found throughout university buildings on campuses in Allendale, Grand Rapids, Holland, Muskegon, Traverse City, and Detroit.

The Digital Collections database contains a selection of photographs, correspondence, diaries, interviews, and publications from the holdings of the Libraries' Special Collections & University Archives, and other University entities.[[97]](#cite_note-dcgvsu-97)

The Dorothy A. Johnson Collection is a collection on philanthropy, volunteerism, and nonprofit leadership. It is considered to be one of the most comprehensive in the United States and is the only one of its kind in Michigan.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Student life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Kirkhof2.jpg/250px-Kirkhof2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kirkhof2.jpg)

A panoramic view of the Kirkhof lawn at GVSU-Allendale campus

The Office of Student Life at GVSU is in the [Kirkhof Center](/wiki/Kirkhof_Center "Kirkhof Center") near the center of the Allendale campus. Its offices are home to the Community Service Learning Center, Fraternity and Sorority Life, Laker Leadership Programs, Major Campus Events and Traditions, Student Organizations, and the Transitions Orientation Program.[[99]](#cite_note-studentlife-99) As of fall 2016, GVSU has over 486 student-run organizations.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) Student organizations include categories in, but are not limited to, Academic and Professional, Student Life Sports, Cultural, Honorary, Interfaith and Religious, Media, Performing Arts, Service and Advocacy, and Special Interests.[[99]](#cite_note-studentlife-99)

### Housing and residential life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Housing and residential life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/70/Connection_Commons.JPG/250px-Connection_Commons.JPG)](/wiki/File:Connection_Commons.JPG)

The Connection Commons at GVSU-Allendale Campus

Grand Valley State University is home to 29 living centers (residence halls) and three on-campus apartment complexes on its main Allendale campus, and two residence halls on its Pew Grand Rapids campus, totaling 6,068 beds.[[31]](#cite_note-thecampuses-31)[[100]](#cite_note-gvprojhousing-100) GVSU also has six individual dining halls on campus for students and faculty. These dining halls are housed in five facilities with five halls being on the Allendale campus and one on the Pew campus.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

### Greek life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Greek life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/GVSU_Henry_Hall_Arch.JPG/250px-GVSU_Henry_Hall_Arch.JPG)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Henry_Hall_Arch.JPG)

Henry Hall Arch at GVSU-Allendale campus

GVSU has 30 fraternities and sororities.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) As of the winter of 2016, the GVSU fraternity and sorority community consisted of 1,600 undergraduate members, representing approximately 7.8% of the undergraduate population.[[102]](#cite_note-fratsoror-102)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Media")]

GVSU has a variety of media outlets offered to its campuses.

The *[Grand Valley Lanthorn](/wiki/Grand_Valley_Lanthorn "Grand Valley Lanthorn")* is the student-run newspaper, published on Mondays and Thursdays during the academic year. Copies of the paper are free and available at both the Allendale and Grand Rapids campuses in designated newsstands and online. Until the fall of 2006, the paper was only published once per week. 8,000 copies of the paper are published per issue totaling 16,000 copies per week.[[103]](#cite_note-aboutus-103)

GVSU has several electronic media outlets including three radio stations and three television stations. The university owns and operates its own [Public Broadcasting Service](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") (PBS) [Public television](/wiki/Public_television "Public television") station [WGVU-TV](/wiki/WGVU-TV "WGVU-TV") in Allendale, Michigan and [WGVK-TV](/wiki/WGVU-TV "WGVU-TV"), its full-time satellite station in [Kalamazoo, Michigan](/wiki/Kalamazoo,_Michigan "Kalamazoo, Michigan"). In addition, GVSU also operates a [National Public Radio](/wiki/National_Public_Radio "National Public Radio") (NPR) station in [WGVU-FM](/wiki/WGVU-FM "WGVU-FM") with the same call letters, which feature a mixture of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), and news, including local and NPR programming.[[104]](#cite_note-gvu-104) WCKS ("The Whale") is the student-run radio station, broadcasting over the internet and Tunein.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Grand Valley TV (GVTV) is the student-run television station on channel 46.1[[106]](#cite_note-106) on the university cable system, while uploading its content to YouTube.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Music")]

The music program at Grand Valley State University offers various performing ensembles, including 3 performing bands, an orchestra, several small performance ensembles, and the 230-piece Laker Marching Band and adjunct athletic bands.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) The Performing Arts Center (PAC) houses numerous rehearsal spaces, classrooms, labs, offices, practice halls, two dance studios, a theatre workshop, and the Louis Armstrong Theatre, along with the new Sherman Van Solkema recital hall.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

The New Music Ensemble (NME)[[111]](#cite_note-nme-111) has released two critically acclaimed CDs, the first a recording of [Steve Reich](/wiki/Steve_Reich "Steve Reich")'s *[Music for 18 Musicians](/wiki/Music_for_18_Musicians "Music for 18 Musicians")*,[[112]](#cite_note-18musiciansNPR-112) which appeared on the *Billboard* Classical Crossover chart, and the second entitled *In C Remixed*,[[113]](#cite_note-Kosman-113)[[114]](#cite_note-Walls-114)[[115]](#cite_note-WashPost-115) a remix project of [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley")'s [In C](/wiki/In_C "In C"), which featured the work of some of the top electronic composers and DJs in the world, including [Todd Reynolds](/wiki/Todd_Reynolds_(musician) "Todd Reynolds (musician)"), [Michael Lowenstern](/wiki/Michael_Lowenstern "Michael Lowenstern"), and Pulitzer prize winner [David Lang](/wiki/David_Lang_(composer) "David Lang (composer)"). The NME performed at Le Poisson Rouge in New York City on November 2, 2009[[116]](#cite_note-Kozinn-116) and previously at the 2007 [Bang on a Can](/wiki/Bang_on_a_Can "Bang on a Can") festival.[[117]](#cite_note-bangcan-117)

Grand Valley's trumpet ensemble has been a top competitor in the [National Trumpet Competition](/wiki/National_Trumpet_Competition "National Trumpet Competition") for nearly a decade, placing first in the ensemble division in 2006 and 2008.[[118]](#cite_note-ensemble_wins_national_championship-118) The ensemble has commissioned four pieces by composer [Erik Morales](/wiki/Erik_Morales "Erik Morales") since 2005, two of which went on to become the winning pieces, and one of which is still unpremiered. The trumpet ensemble has performed twice during the [International Trumpet Guild](/wiki/International_Trumpet_Guild "International Trumpet Guild")'s summer conferences in 2007[[119]](#cite_note-trumpeter_wins_intnl_comp-119) and 2009.[[120]](#cite_note-messiahtrumpet-120)

### Student government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Student government")]

Student government at Grand Valley is formally known as Student Senate.[[121]](#cite_note-121) There are 50 student senators that serve on one of seven different committees. Student Senate offices are in the Kirkhof Center on GVSU's Allendale campus.[[122]](#cite_note-122)

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/GVSU_Little_Mac_Bridge.JPG/250px-GVSU_Little_Mac_Bridge.JPG)](/wiki/File:GVSU_Little_Mac_Bridge.JPG)

Little Mac Bridge at Grand Valley State University-Allendale campus

Sustainability is listed as the seventh of Grand Valley's core values.[[123]](#cite_note-gvsu.edu-123) The institution's Office of Sustainability Practices provides best "practices in administration and campus operations, educational opportunities, student involvement, and community engagement."[[124]](#cite_note-gvsuscdi-124) Notable programs include a [community garden](/wiki/Community_garden "Community garden") near the Allendale campus that also serves as a laboratory for interdisciplinary learning called the Sustainable Agriculture Project[[125]](#cite_note-125) and the Sustainability Reinvestment Fund distributed in the form of mini-grants to students and staff with ideas that can improve the ecological footprint of the campus and community.[[123]](#cite_note-gvsu.edu-123)

The university has created a formal climate action plan and set a goal to reduce its 2006 level of greenhouse gas emissions 20% by 2020.[[126]](#cite_note-gvsusei-126) The university's sustainability efforts were awarded a "A−" by the [Sustainable Endowments Institute](/wiki/Campus_Carbon_Neutrality#Sustainable_Endowments_Institute_&_the_College_Sustainability_Report_Card "Campus Carbon Neutrality") for 2011.[[126]](#cite_note-gvsusei-126) Grand Valley's overall sustainability grade is the highest amongst the nine Michigan universities that were surveyed and the 28th best in the nation.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) In other rankings GVSU was placed as high as 16th in the world and tenth in the United States for its sustainability efforts and environment-friendly university management by Universitas Indonesia GreenMetric World University Ranking for 2011.[[129]](#cite_note-greenmetric-129)

The university is home to eleven Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design ([LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design")) buildings or building additions.[[126]](#cite_note-gvsusei-126) Several more buildings including the new Mary Idema Pew Library are in construction or planning stages to be LEED certified as well.[[130]](#cite_note-130) All new construction and major renovations at GVSU must meet LEED standards required by the university.[[126]](#cite_note-gvsusei-126) As of 2019 there have been 25 LEED-certified projects completed at Grand Valley.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) $2.2 million is saved annually on Grand Valley's energy bill through a combination of energy conservation efforts and LEED construction projects.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3)

The Student Environmental Coalition or SEC, is the student-run sustainability organization on campus. The mission of the group is "Helping to engage students at Grand Valley's campus to be conscious citizens by promotion of environmental awareness."[[131]](#cite_note-131)

Additionally, several academic programs offer the study of sustainability. Those housed in the College of Liberal Arts and Sciences include Geography and Sustainable Planning (major and minor) and Natural Resource Management (major and minor). Those housed in the Brooks College of Interdisciplinary Studies include the Liberal Studies major and the Environmental Studies minor.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Grand Valley hosted meetings relating to sustainability, including a summit in which various sustainability issues were explored by university officials and local experts in April 2010,[[133]](#cite_note-grpresssustainability-133)[[134]](#cite_note-hcpssustainability-134) and a meeting of the Michigan Great Lakes Wind Council in July 2010 to discuss offshore wind energy generation.[[135]](#cite_note-ghtribwind-135)[[136]](#cite_note-opwind-136)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Valley_State_University&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Grand Valley State Lakers](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_Lakers "Grand Valley State Lakers")

See also: [Grand Valley State Lakers football](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_Lakers_football "Grand Valley State Lakers football")

The Grand Valley State Lakers are the intercollegiate athletic teams of Grand Valley State University.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) The GVSU Lakers compete at the [NCAA Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") level and offer 20 varsity sports including 11 for women and 9 for men.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) The university participates in and is a founding member of the [Great Lakes Intercollegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Conference "Great Lakes Intercollegiate Athletic Conference") (GLIAC).[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) Grand Valley's varsity athletic teams have won 28 national championships in several sports. GVSU has also won the prestigious National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics [(NACDA) Directors' Cup](/wiki/NACDA_Director%27s_Cup "NACDA Director's Cup") for NCAA Division II schools fourteen times: in 2004 thru 2011, 2014 thru 2017, 2019, and 2022. The Lakers also have five second-place finishes in 2002, 2003, 2012, 2013 and 2018.[[137]](#cite_note-137) The cup is awarded to the top athletic programs based on overall team national finishes. Grand Valley is the first college east of the Mississippi River to win the Director's Cup for NCAA Division II.

The official mascot of Grand Valley State is Louie the Laker.[[3]](#cite_note-quickfacts-3) "[GVSU Victory!](/wiki/Grand_Valley_State_Lakers#Fight_song "Grand Valley State Lakers")", which is sometimes referred to as "Grand Valley Victory", is the fight song for the Grand Valley State University Lakers.[[138]](#cite_note-marchingband-138)
